file_verdicts.summary: Executes untrusted Python code during source fetch

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/gnome-browser-connector-git.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-urhcrz7w...
Cloned repository to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-urhcrz7w
Analyzing gnome-browser-connector-git AUR package (working in /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-urhcrz7w) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>UNSAFE</decision>
<details>
The `pkgver()` function executes `python3 -c` with code that imports and runs a version identifier from `gnome_browser_connector.version`. The issue arises because the `source` is a git repository that is cloned before `pkgver()` is evaluated, meaning the Python code being imported comes from the untrusted upstream source and is executed during the sourcing of the PKGBUILD.

Even though `makepkg --noprepare --nobuild --nodeps` does not run `prepare()`, `build()`, or `package()`, it *does* source the PKGBUILD and execute `pkgver()` to determine the package version. The command substitution inside `pkgver()` runs arbitrary Python code from the cloned git repo, specifically `gnome_browser_connector/version.py`, which could contain malicious logic such as network exfiltration or execution of payloads.

This constitutes a supply-chain risk during source fetching and PKGBUILD parsing: simply running `makepkg --printsrcinfo` or `makepkg --nobuild` triggers a clone and then executes upstream Python code in the context of the build environment. Since this behavior involves running executable code from an untrusted source during a phase that should only parse metadata, it is unsafe.

Snippet:
```
python3 -c 'from gnome_browser_connector.version import __version__; print(__version__, end="");'
```
</details>
<summary>Executes untrusted Python code during source fetch</summary>
</security_assessment>

Since this behavior involves running executable code from an untrusted source during a phase that should only parse metadata, it is unsafe.

The source array fetches from the official GNOME GitLab instance (`git+https://gitlab.gnome.org/nE0sIghT/gnome-browser-connector.git`), which is the expected upstream for this package. The `md5sums` are `SKIP`, standard for VCS packages. No top-level command substitutions, obfuscation, or external downloads are present. Running `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo` will only clone the source and source the PKGBUILD, without executing any malicious code.
</details>

LLM auditresponse for service.py:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `service.py` implements a standard DBus service for handling notifications within the GNOME desktop environment. It uses GI (GObject Introspection) bindings to interact with GLib and Gio, creating simple actions for notification creation, click handling, and action responses. There is no obfuscated code, no network requests, no execution of external commands, and no file operations outside of expected application behavior. The code is consistent with the stated purpose of the gnome-browser-connector package, which facilitates browser integration with GNOME. No evidence of supply-chain attack or malicious behavior found.
</details>
<summary>
Standard GNOME service code, no malicious behavior detected.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for meson.build:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This is a standard Meson build configuration file for the `gnome-browser-connector` package. It defines project metadata, locates the Python 3 installation, and installs scripts, Python modules, browser connector manifests (for Chromium and Firefox), a DBus service file, a desktop file, and icons. All operations are limited to the package's own installation directories (`bindir`, `sysconfdir`, `libdir`, `datadir`) and use only Meson built-in functions (`configure_file`, `install_subdir`, `install_data`, `foreach`). The only external command executed is `python3 -c` to retrieve the package version from its own module, which is a normal and expected practice. There are no network requests, no obfuscated code, no dangerous commands (eval, base64, curl, wget), and no file operations outside the package's scope. The file follows standard packaging practices and contains no evidence of malicious or supply-chain attack behavior.
</details>
<summary>Standard Meson build file, no security issues.</summary>
</security_assessment>
